sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A pain-perception experiment applied the same warm stimulus to participants in two contexts. In the neutral context, participants were told the stimulus was a standard sensation test. In the threat context, they were told the same stimulus was part of a study of tissue sensitivity. Participants in the threat context rated the stimulus as more painful and showed more facial tension, even though the device temperature was identical. Researchers used the finding to discuss top-down influences on perception.
questions.stem: What was the key manipulation in the experiment?

choices.A: The meaning or context assigned to the same stimulus
choices.B: The physical temperature of the stimulus
choices.C: The number of pain receptors in the skin

explanations.A: Correct. The device temperature stayed identical, while the instructions changed the perceived meaning of the stimulus. That makes contextual interpretation the key manipulation.
explanations.B: The passage states that the device temperature was identical.
explanations.C: Pain receptor number was not manipulated or measured.
explanations.D: Age group was not described as the manipulated variable.

questions.stem: The higher pain ratings in the threat context best illustrate what?

explanations.D: Correct. Top-down processing occurs when expectations, beliefs, or context shape perception. Here, the same physical stimulus felt more painful when framed as threatening.
explanations.A: A purely bottom-up account would predict identical ratings because the stimulus was physically identical.
explanations.B: Operant extinction is not the process in the pain-rating difference.
explanations.C: Social loafing concerns group effort, not pain perception.

questions.stem: Why is facial tension relevant to the interpretation?

choices.B: It suggests the context affected behavioral response as well as verbal ratings
choices.A: It proves the device temperature increased
choices.C: It shows participants could not understand instructions

explanations.B: Correct. Facial tension provides converging behavioral evidence that threat framing changed the pain experience or response. It makes the effect less dependent on self-report alone.
explanations.A: The passage says temperature was identical, so tension does not prove a temperature change.
explanations.C: There is no indication that participants misunderstood instructions.
explanations.D: A top-down influence does not mean the pain experience was fake.

questions.stem: A clinician applying the finding should most likely conclude what?

choices.C: Patient expectations and communication can shape pain experiences
choices.A: Pain reports are useless because context matters
choices.B: Clinicians should always use threatening language

explanations.C: Correct. The study suggests that how a sensation is framed can affect pain ratings. Clinicians should communicate accurately and calmly because expectation can modulate real experiences of pain.
explanations.A: Context effects mean reports need thoughtful interpretation, not dismissal.
explanations.B: Threatening language would likely worsen pain or distress.
explanations.D: The identical stimulus produced different ratings, so tissue input is not the only influence.

choices.D: The same sensory input can be experienced differently depending on cognitive context
choices.B: Top-down processing eliminates the need for nociceptors
choices.C: Pain perception cannot be studied experimentally

explanations.D: Correct. The experiment held the stimulus constant while changing context, supporting the conclusion that cognition can alter perception. It does not show tissue damage or deny the role of sensory pathways.
explanations.A: The passage does not report tissue damage.
explanations.B: Top-down influences modulate sensory input; they do not replace nociception.
explanations.C: The passage itself describes an experiment studying pain perception.

questions.passage: A medical decision-making class presented students with two screening scenarios. In both, a test had 90% sensitivity and 90% specificity. In Scenario A, the condition prevalence was 10%; in Scenario B, prevalence was 1%. Students estimated the probability that a patient with a positive test truly had the condition. The correct positive predictive value was much higher in Scenario A than in Scenario B, but many students gave similar estimates for both scenarios because they focused on test accuracy and neglected base rates.
questions.stem: Which scenario should have the higher positive predictive value?

choices.C: Both scenarios must have identical positive predictive value
choices.D: Neither scenario has any true positives

explanations.A: Correct. With the same sensitivity and specificity, the condition with higher prevalence will produce a higher proportion of true positives among all positive tests. Scenario A therefore has the higher positive predictive value.
explanations.B: The lower prevalence in Scenario B means more positive tests will be false positives relative to true positives.
explanations.C: Equal sensitivity and specificity do not guarantee equal positive predictive value when prevalence differs.
explanations.D: Both scenarios can have true positives; their proportions differ.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Approximate true-positive and false-positive counts for two screening scenarios with identical sensitivity and specificity but different prevalence.

questions.stem: Students who ignored prevalence were showing what bias?

explanations.B: Correct. Base-rate neglect occurs when people underweight prior probability or prevalence information when judging likelihood. The students focused on test accuracy while neglecting prevalence.
explanations.A: The bystander effect concerns helping in emergencies.
explanations.C: Habituation is decreased response after repeated exposure.
explanations.D: Attachment insecurity concerns caregiver relationships, not probability judgment.

questions.stem: Why does lower prevalence reduce positive predictive value?

choices.C: False positives can outnumber true positives when the condition is rare
choices.A: Sensitivity becomes zero in rare conditions
choices.B: Specificity no longer exists when prevalence is low
choices.D: All positive tests are automatically true

explanations.C: Correct. When a condition is rare, even a fairly specific test may generate false positives among the large number of unaffected people. That lowers the proportion of positive tests that are true positives.
explanations.A: Sensitivity is a property of the test among people with the condition and does not become zero just because prevalence is low.
explanations.B: Specificity still applies; its false-positive implications are especially important in low-prevalence settings.
explanations.D: Positive tests can be false positives, especially when prevalence is low.

questions.stem: A clinician teaching patients about screening should most likely do what?

choices.A: Explain both test accuracy and condition prevalence
choices.B: Report sensitivity only and ignore prevalence
choices.C: Tell patients that a positive screen is always a diagnosis
choices.D: Avoid discussing false positives because they are impossible

explanations.A: Correct. The passage shows that test accuracy alone can mislead. Clear communication should include prevalence and the possibility of false positives so patients understand what a positive screening result means.
explanations.B: Sensitivity alone omits specificity and prevalence, both needed for interpretation.
explanations.C: A positive screen may require confirmation; it is not always a diagnosis.
explanations.D: False positives are central to why positive predictive value changes.

questions.stem: Which learning objective is best supported by the scenario?

choices.D: Predictive values depend on both test characteristics and the population being tested
choices.B: Prevalence matters only for treatment and never for screening
choices.C: A single test result contains no probabilistic information

explanations.D: Correct. The same sensitivity and specificity produce different positive predictive values when prevalence changes. This teaches that screening interpretation depends on the test and the base rate in the tested population.
explanations.A: Sensitivity and specificity are meaningful but incomplete for patient-level interpretation.
explanations.B: Prevalence strongly affects screening interpretation, not only treatment decisions.
explanations.C: A test result is probabilistic information that must be interpreted in context.
